turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well the First Circuit theory really was that these people were so totally illegal that they had to be acquitted, wasn't it?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I think that's correct. And the Court below stands alone in reaching that anomalous result.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or was it that they were so well organ­ ized in their enterprise that --

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I believe the holding of the Court below was that enterprises that are entirely and completely illegal are not within the scope of the principal anti-racketeering statute in the federal criminal code. That strikes us as an anomalous result, and every other Court of Appeals to consider the issue has concluded that RICO is not restricted legitimate and illegitimate the Respondent and amici seek to insert into the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under the First Circuit theory, on which side of the line would fall an enterprise that had an appar­ ently legitimate front, that is? a truck line, or whatever, but was --

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Well, I think that's difficult to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- was engaged 75 percent or 90 percent in shooting for hire, killing for hire, arson for hire and extortion and loan sharking. Where would the First Circuit case take us?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I don't think one knows with certainty, because the meaning of the terms legitimate and illegitimate are by no means self evident, and neither the statute nor the would lead to distinctions according to degree of legitimacy that are entirely unrelated to Congress' purpose to eradicate organized crime.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In any event, it's common ground there has to be an enterprise.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: It is common ground that there has to be an enterprise.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And enterprise is defined in the statute how, where is it?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Relevant here, enterprise is defined as an entity consisting of a group of individuals associated in fact. That's the statutory definition of enterprise under i

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well Mr. Levy, is there a difference between an illegal 'enterprise and the coverage under the Act of a group of persons who commit a pattern of racketeering activities, that is two predicate acts, is there a difference between the two?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Well, if I understand the question cor­ rectly I believe there is. An enterprise is an entity as in this case, a group of individuals associated in fact.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well no, what I'm trying to get at is what distinguishes a group of persons who commit a pattern of racketeering activities, that is, two predicate acts from an illegal enterprise?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: The enterprise is an entity looking to

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I believe what that means is that an individual may be an enterprise, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So one person could be prosecuted under RICO?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I think that is conceivable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If he were carrying on an illegitimate -- the illegitimate business of bank robbery?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Let me say that is not at issue in this case, and as far as I know, the government has never brought a case --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under the statutory definition do you submit that he could?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's this got to do with a similar

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: If two people --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- enterprise includes any individual this says.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that means that any person who is a repetitive offender can be prosecuted under RICO, does it?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Well, I think not necessarily. It says

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Yes. I think that a single individual of RICO.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: You can organize in the sense that you engage in a conduct in an organized and recurring --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well an individual operates individually --

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- not through an organization.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: That is correct in some circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well in every circumstance, isn't it? would fall under RICO.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: That's a different problem.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Say he commits two bank robberies, one on February 1st, one on March 1st . Why doesn't that violate the statute?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: It might well violate --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under 1962(c), isn't that directly covered?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: It might well violate the statute. But as I say, it's a little hard to know since the government has never brought a prosecution against a single individual, to the best of my knowledge, and the provision simply hasn't been construed. i

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well the statute does define an enter­ prise as any individual, doesn't it?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: It does. And a prosecution could be brought --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then certainly you could, I suppose one person could infiltrate another individual, I mean, if you couldn't be prosecuted for your own activities you could subvert an individual proprietorship of another.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: You might be able to, and that might fall within either subsections (a) or (b), depending on the method of infiltration.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Kind of like the aliens?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: After your answers to my colleagues'

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well I know, but you've already told us an individual may be an enterprise, a group of persons committing two predicate acts may be an enterprise.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: They may be, but they are not necessarily.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then I still ask you what -- how are either of those distinguished from "an illegal enterprise"? Aren't they illegal enterprises?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Perhaps I didn't understand the question. If the association in fact is formed solely to commit illegal acts and I think that's what the Court of Appeals had in mind when it spoke of an illegal or an illegitimate enter­ prise. It's hard to know how that would be applied in a particular case to conclude --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well of course, what I'm getting at is, HR. LEVY: We believe it is without merit; that there be an enterprise or an entity and there is still the requirement that there be certain criminal acts. It may well be true, and this may have been what the Court of Appeals had in mind, that the government's proof in a particular case will be much the same to show the pattern element of the offense and to show the enterprise element of the offense; the fact that people commit a series of offenses together may be a grounds for the jury to infer that they had joined together to form an association in fact that would satisfy the requirement of an enterprise. But the fact that the government's proof may be a legal entity"?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: ' Well I think if you had some informal group of individuals who formed a civic association or a bridge club or something like that, there would be no entity in the legal sense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This even reaches bridge clubs 5 RICO?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Excuse me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This even reaches bridge clubs, does it?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Not necessarily; they would still have to commit a pattern of racketeering activities and I assume most bridge players would not do that. But they might well be an enterprise --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But even as my brother White just suggested, what if they play for money? would be covered here?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I think that's absolutely correct, and I think that's -- certainly incongruous to think that Congress in passing the Racketeer. Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act didn't mean to take into account the Cosa Nostra or the Mafia in the prohibitions of the Act. The reading of RICO I've just suggested to encompass illegitimate as well as legitimate enterprises, is fully consistent with and supported by the legislative history of the statute and the statute's purpose. The legislative history nowhere indicates that Congress intended to limit the statute exclusively to the infiltration and corruption of prohibited by other criminal laws.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: ' I don't doubt that Congress could have drafted the statute in many ways, but I think there's. --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It wouldn't have been totally irrational for Congress to say well, since those are covered by existing laws, the new law will try to reach something else.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: By definition, any prosecution under RICO has to involve the violation of some other statutes. Congress passed RICO to supplement those existing statutes because of what it found to be inadequacies --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And because of its primary concern, and eradicated. And it was not designed for some narrow and technical statute in the manner that Respondent suggests.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Levy, there are a number of kind of ambiguous phrases in the briefs and so on, yet as you pointed out illegitimate and legitimate -- pose questions in the brief of what is organized crime as opposed to unorganized crime?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Or perhaps disorganized crime?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or disorganized crime.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: The statute and the legislative history don't define that. We think in general, Congress used that term in a rather broad sense, as you've suggested, to mean organized criminal activities as well as in the narrower sense to mean the mob or the Cosa Nostra, organized crime in the vernacular sense. I think they were concerned about racketeer­ sense view, and on the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For buying protection, and that sort of thing?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: Well I think it had in mind those things, but it didn't by any means limit itself to that. And the statute, if I may point out, does not require that the defen­ dant need be a member of an organized crime or organized criminal activity, much as in Culbert, the Court held that the Hobbs Act was not limited in that way. The statutory statement of findings in the legis­ lative history of RICO plainly revealed Congress' awareness of and concern over organized crime's wholly illegal activities, and nothing suggests that Congress intended these activities constitute the economic foundation of organized crime and Thus even if the sole purpose of the statute were to pre­ predicate acts of racketeering were related to, that would simply be in contrast sporadic or isolated criminal activity and that's the very reason why the Court of Appeals was incorrect in suggesting that our interpretation of the statute rendered the statutory element enterprise redundant to the term pattern. They think that is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But how about 1961 Subsection 5, where it defines pattern of racketeering activity as at least two acts of racketeering activity, one of which occurred after the effective date of this Chapter and the last of which occurred within ten years, including any period of imprison­ ment. Wouldn't my brother Brennan's example come under that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, but your answer to my brother

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That then would be an enterprise.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: That's right. Now, it's --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: ' But the hypothetical I gave You-, they were just isolated acts with no agreement between them to make this a regular pattern of living.

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's take another one. A single individual who talks to no one, but simply goes around to a series of pharmacies and says to the proprietor, I want to buy x-number of certain drugs, cocaine and some others, without a prescription and you either give them to me or your windows will be broken as fast as you can replace them. And he goes around to ten establishments and gets the cocaine and sells it. Is that an enterprise?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: I think they would, although I don't mean to suggest that bridge clubs would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well if you could win your case without conceding that the individual could be, himself, prosecuted?

turns.name: LEVY
turns.text: That's correct. I repeat that our case doesn't involve a single individual, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But your argument does .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even if you put the individual aside on the grounds that it was silly, then it would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your argument involves that statute, that part of the statutory definition or more accurately, your opponent's argument, because that implies that the individual has to be -- is engaged in a legitimate enterprise, and he has to be infiltrated by criminal activity. vidual or a sporadic and isolated pattern of crime. The Court of Appeals was also concerned with respect to the redundancy of the statute about applying the criminal provisions of Sections 1962(a) and (b) and the civil pro­ visions of Section 1964 to illegitimate enterprises. We have discussed that in the brief, and we don't think there is any in­ consistency or redundancy in applying the statute that way, and indeed the application of those provisions could well serve Congress' purpose to eradicate organized crime by enabling the government to prosecute a defendant who used racketeering income, for example, to establish an illegal court decisions as Elliott. And I'd like to mention, in that regard, what RICO does not do and was not intended by Congress to do. The RICO statute neither by its terms nor in its legislative history, ever did purport to allow a single prosecution of a defendant for all his disparate and unrelated and otherwise non-joinable crimes. Also, in enacting RICO, Congress did not intend to extend vicarious liability beyond the limits of traditional conspiracy law to permit mass trials. What RICO does do and what it was intended to do, legitimate business involved, and then there is some illegit­ imate penetration of that, is that it?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Yes sir. What I'm saying is, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would have to be totally illegitimate from the outset?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: No sir, not under the definition, and under the precise restricted purpose that Congress had in enacting this one section. And I can perhaps best underline that, Mr. Chief Justice Burger, if we think of the enterprise that we're protecting as the victim, not the criminal, at the heart of the split in the circuit is the distinction between treat the enterprise as a victim. And this satisfies the as a whole and to make every element within the statute have significance and not be redundant, we consider --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Wdll, why does your distinction between victim and criminal enterprise itself necessarily answer our problem? Because is it not conceivable that you might have say, a neighborhood numbers game, or a neighborhood prostitution business, something like that. And a larger criminal element comes in and by racketeering activities takes over that more or less local enterprise. Why doesn't the statute apply there and there the one taken over would be the victim?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: There are other titles within the enterprise.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Well, the reason that analogy is helpful is that if we follow the Elliott definition and say that all illegal enterprises are included, then we have no cohesive thought to apply the conspiracy concept to. According to Elliott, the essential nature of the plan was to associate for the purpose of making money. And rather than the wheels and the chains, we have an amoeba-like infrastructure that controls the secret criminal network. Until Elliott, defining RICO to include so-called illegal enterprises, that is, to include an individual who does activities, -- excuse me, I lost my train of thought. enterprise includes any individual, partnership, corporation, association or other legal entity and any union or group of individuals associated in fact, although not a legal entity.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Yes sir, it does make a distinction as the First Circuit and other courts in dissenting opinions have pointed out. Under the rule of ejusdem generis, that -- in interpreting the statute, you apply that canon of construction. Also, when considered with the other elements in the statute, to give enterprise independent meaning it would have to only refer to legitimate business rather than illegitimate. -- the first category under subsection (4) is any individual, partnership, and then the whole array, seriatim. Why weren't they trying to get at this one strong-armed extortionist?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: - They weren't because -- because they stated that with this particular title they were directing the protection to the victims of -- interstate businesses that were victims of organized criminal activity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And an individual could be a victim, if he were an individual entrepreneur.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: He could be a victim, yes sir, if he's involved in legitimate --

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: That's absolutely correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: ' It's illegal activity.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: It's illegal activity, there has to be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I was just starting with the first category, I was going to take you through the rest of them.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: The loner --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it not .covered if it's illegal from its inception?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: If the loner in the first instance, by robbing the drug store intends only to rob the drugstore, he's not covered. If he intends by robbing the drugstore two separate times, to make the drugstore owner give him a piece under the definition, you assume as I understand it, the first listed things like 'partnership, corporation, association, would necessarily be legitimate. But is it not possible that a corporation could be organized and do nothing but deal in stolen goods or stolen securities or something like that, and be wholly illegitimate. Would you say that even a corporate, a corporation, so organized and totally illegitimate would not be an enterprise within the meaning of the statute?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: That, sir, was precisely the reason that liberal construction provision was submitted, I suggest. 904(a). And that, I suggest that Congress intended to mean that you liberally construe who are the victims. Congress wanted to protect as many forms of legitimate endeavors as pos­ sible, so to effectuate the remedial purposes of 904(a) you it doesn't matter in the long run.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't see, personally, I don't see how urging us to apply broad principles and broad construction to criminal statutes helps a defendant. You kind of lose me on that one.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Well, Mr. Justice, I'm not suggesting that, i was suggesting that the liberal construction pro­ vision of 904(a) should be applied only to the definition liberally construe who are the victims. So that, when you have the definition statute -- define broadly. But when it comes to the Rule of Lenity, when that's applied to a wrongdoer, the criminal, the one acting rather than the one acted upon, no. And by the approach that we take, you protect the Rule of Lenity, and you avoid the due process implications mentioned in Dunn v. the United States, where Mr. Justice Marshall said, writing for the majority of this Court, as a matter of fact for the unanimous court, this practice, that is the Rule of Lenity, is not merely a convenient maxim of statutory construction, rather, it is rooted in fundamental principles of due process- Related to that argument •, and particularly related to what we consider probably the most important case to be Court. In our view, the Justice Department's contemporaneous rather than current interpretation offers the more plausible reading of the subcommittee's intent. At the legislative hearings on RICO, the Attorney General, the chief of the criminal division, everybody, said that the purpose of RICO -- again, applying the scalpel as part of an overall plan, is to cut out the cancer of racketeering activity on legit­ imate interstate businesses.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Hr. Wall, I gather from what you said much earlier, you read 1962(c) as addressed to a situation in which some employee or somebody associated with a legitimate enterprise, sets about conducting that legitimate enterprise in business through a pattern of racketeering activity or that Congress was more interested in protecting a victim trom -

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Than getting the wrongdoer --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --infiltration or otherwise --

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Title 10. They specifically went after the repeat offender, and Title 8,• they specifically went after another aspect of organized crime, that's precisely the point I'm making, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's collection of unlawful debt?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: A gambling debt, I assume.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or usurious debts?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Certainly in 1962 -- goes after some reparation statute or a victim of crime statute. It in itself makes certain acts criminal that weren't otherwise made criminal.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Right. That's a fact, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, how do you square your previous statement --

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Well, excuse me. I didn't mean to interrupt you, Mr. Chief Justice. It doesn't -- the acts themselves were criminal previously, and they are otherwise criminal, however; when they are committed in this pattern -- two separate acts, to do this act, attack the victim that we want to protect, it's a ---

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Traditionally? debts, gambling debts, any other kind, and that's all they do.

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: I say they are not covered.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean, they can't be prosecuted under this statute?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Under this particular statute, RICO, it was not designed, it's -- it was not designed, and doesn't make internal sense, and it destroys the traditional con­ spiracy and joinder rules if it is applied to that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Destroys them or supplements them?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: I suggest, Mr. Chief Justice Burger, most respectfully, it destroys them. And it changes --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't this one of the broadest nets that Congress has ever thrown out to catch criminal activity? that. Members of the Court, it has been suggested that even if this Court 'does agree with our view of the scope of RICO, that my client should nevertheless have the First Circuit reversal, the change, because there was harmless error And our argument is, of course, that there was not harmless error, that he was joined with other defendants he should not have been, and that he was joined in a number of criminal charges against him, that without the RICO expansion of con- spiracy vehicle he could never have been tried together for.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you ever seek a severance?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In the trial court?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: After conviction?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about the sentences? On the sub­ stantive counts, didn't he not get sentences concurrent with the sentence under the RICO count?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: He did, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: . But he had -- how was he prejudiced? The sentences under the substantive counts stand, don't they? counts, and might have wanted to testify but was not able to because of the drug charge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the United States urge harmless error in the Court of Appeals?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And decided if against them?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they held as a matter of law that reversal was required, regardless of whether it was harmless error or not, didn't they?

turns.name: WALL
turns.text: Except for this: I suggest the intima­ tion is, and the fact is, that in these circumstances there's of law, that in other words, one cannot always segregate the technique from the 'substance, or the form from the reality. I'm suggesting also, members of this Court, that in your decision in Dunn that living, wonderful principle of our law was.again upheld where the Court of Appeals in the Tenth Circuit said, it's obvious there was a variant, sure, but the guy lied under oath, even if it was in the lawyer's office, so let the conviction stand. Oh no, this Court, in a unanimous opinion written by Chief Justice -- or rather, I'm sorry, by Mr. Justice Marshall, stated: the jury well -- may well have reached the same verdict had the prosecution built its case properly. But the fallacy may be shown by the Ninth Circuit's decision in the Zemek case cited in our reply brief, where the owner of a tavern advanced his tavern business through extortion and murder of his competitors. In that case, the enterprise was a tavern and that was advanced rather than being the victim of the racketeering activity. above matter was submitted.)
